1: The staff-test episode follows immediately after the plague — YHWH's judgment has silenced but not persuaded the grumblers (v.12-13 will show their despair). The new revelation formula opens a different kind of divine response to the authority challenge: where ch.16 demonstrated YHWH's negative verdict through judgment, ch.17 will demonstrate YHWH's positive designation through miraculous growth. The two responses together — judgment and vindication — constitute the complete divine answer to Korah's challenge.
2: One staff per ancestral house (beit av) — twelve staffs for twelve tribes, one for each leader (nasi). The staff (matteh, H4294) is the same word as 'tribe' in Hebrew — the leader's staff is the embodiment of tribal identity. The test uses the most ordinary object of leadership to carry the most extraordinary sign: from a dead piece of wood, YHWH will produce life.
3: Aaron's name is written on the Levitical staff — one staff for Levi as for every other tribe, but with Aaron specifically named. The naming individualizes the test: it is not Levitical authority in general that will be vindicated but Aaron's specifically. The staff-writing is an act of ownership and designation: the name on the staff identifies whose claim YHWH will adjudicate. The twelve staffs, with names, function as a kind of written oracle that YHWH will answer.
4: 'Place them in the tent of meeting before the covenant document' — lifnei ha'edut. The staffs are placed in YHWH's immediate presence: the space before the ark and the tablets of the covenant is the most holy site in the Israelite world. The test's site is deliberately chosen: the divine sign will emerge from within YHWH's own dwelling space. No human can manipulate the result; the staffs are placed before YHWH and left for him to act upon.
5: 'The man I choose — his staff will sprout.' Ufarach matteh ha'ish asher evchar bo. The sign is specific: growth. Not decay, not fire, not splitting — but the biological sign of life: parach (H6524, blossom, sprout, break out). In Hebrew idiom, parach is associated with renewal, the righteous flourishing (Ps 92:12; Isa 35:1-2). YHWH will demonstrate his choice through the sign of life emerging from death — from a staff that has been cut from its tree and is, by rights, dead wood. 'I will rid myself of the Israelites' grumbling against you' — the sign is explicitly directed at the grumbling problem identified throughout the wilderness narrative.
6: Moses collects twelve staffs — one per leader, with Aaron's for Levi. The staffs are ordinary leaders' staffs: the same kind that every tribal nasi would carry. The ordinariness of the objects makes the sign more astonishing — YHWH does not use exotic sacred instruments but the everyday symbols of human authority.
7: Moses places the staffs before YHWH in the tent of the covenant (ohel ha'edut). The tent of the covenant/testimony — the tabernacle named for the two tablets of testimony it houses — is the site of the divine act. The placement 'before the LORD' is the key spatial designation: the staffs are within YHWH's direct presence, subject to his action alone.
8:
'The next day Moses went into the tent of the covenant, and there was Aaron's staff — representing the house of Levi — had sprouted: it budded, produced blossoms, and bore ripe almonds.' The Hebrew is remarkably precise: vayifra' vayatzetz tzitz vayyigmal shqedim — it budded, put out blossoms, and produced ripe almonds. Three stages of growth in a single overnight: bud → blossom → ripe fruit. This is not merely germination but accelerated full-cycle growth.

The almond (shaqed, H8247) is the first tree to bloom in the land of Israel — typically in late January or early February, before its leaves appear. Its name in Hebrew (shaqed) is connected to the verb shaqad (H8245, to watch, to be awake/alert). YHWH tells Jeremiah (1:11-12): 'I see a staff of almond (shaqed)... I am watching (shoqed) over my word to perform it.' The almond is the sign of divine watchfulness and initiative — YHWH watching over the covenant to keep it.
9: Moses brings all twelve staffs out for all the Israelites to see. Each leader takes his own staff. The public display of the staffs ensures that the sign is witnessed by the whole community: there can be no doubt about which staff budded, no suspicion of manipulation. The twelve staffs displayed together make the contrast maximally visible — eleven dead sticks and one living tree.
10: 'Put Aaron's staff back before the covenant document, to be kept as a sign against the rebels' — lemishmeret le'ot libn'ei meri. The budded staff joins the covenant document (the tablets) and later the jar of manna (Exod 16:33-34) as items kept within or near the ark as permanent covenantal signs. The staff's preservation links Aaron's priestly authority directly to the Sinai covenant: his priesthood is not a human appointment but a covenant institution. The benei meri (children of rebellion) are warned by the preserved sign that YHWH's choice is irrevocable.
11: 'Moses did so; he did just as the LORD commanded him.' The completion formula. Moses's obedience is the consistent frame of the narrative: regardless of the community's challenge to his authority, he executes YHWH's commands precisely. The formula's brevity stands in contrast to the community's elaborate rebellion; faithfulness to YHWH's commands is simple even when the context is turbulent.
12: 'We are dying, we are perishing, we are all lost.' The community's response to the signs is not repentance but despair: gava'nu avadnu kulanu avadnu — three synonymous declarations of perishing in rapid succession. The threefold expression is a literary-emotional intensification. The signs have demonstrated YHWH's holiness beyond any reasonable doubt, and Israel's response is existential terror: if approaching YHWH is this dangerous, survival seems impossible.
13: 'Anyone who comes near the tabernacle of the LORD shall die. Are we all going to die?' The question — rhetorical, despairing — captures Israel's theological crisis at the end of the Korah narrative. The proximity to YHWH that Korah's company sought by demanding priestly access turns out to be genuinely dangerous. The Israelites' terror is theologically appropriate: drawing near to the holy God without proper mediation is fatal. The cry ends the Korah episode and sets up ch.18's clarification of the mediating structure that makes safe cohabitation with YHWH's holiness possible.
